monochromatic.definition: Using different shades and tints of the same color.
monochromatic.rule: Pair a darker shade with a lighter one for depth.
examples: Navy Blue Shirt with Sky Blue Chinos
Light Grey Hoodie with Dark Charcoal Joggers

complementary.definition: Colors opposite each other on the color wheel.
complementary.rule: Creates a high-contrast, energetic look. Use one as the dominant color and the other as an accent.
examples: Rust Orange Shirt with Teal Pants
Yellow Graphic Tee with Blue Jeans

analogous.definition: Colors next to each other on the color wheel.
analogous.rule: Creates a harmonious and pleasing look. Best for semi-formal or calm casual vibes.
examples: Olive Green Jacket with Yellow-Ochre T-shirt

neutral.definition: Black, white, grey, beige, and navy.
neutral.rule: These go with almost anything. Use them to balance bright colors.

pairingRules.name: The Sandwich Rule
pairingRules.rule: Match the color of your top item with the color of your shoes to 'sandwich' the outfit.
pairingRules.tip: If wearing a black hoodie and black shoes, go with light-colored pants for contrast.

pairingRules.name: Dark vs Light Contrast
pairingRules.rule: Pair a dark top with light bottoms, or vice versa, to create clear silhouette definitions.
pairingRules.tip: Avoid wearing all very dark or all very light colors unless going for a specific monochromatic look.

wedding.male: Indian Ethnic (Kurta/Sherwani) or Formal Suit. Primary focus: Rich fabrics and jewel tones.
wedding.female: Saree, Lehenga, or Anarkali. Primary focus: Embroidery and festive colors (Red, Gold, Pink).

party.male: Smart Casual or Party Wear Shirt. Focus: Prints or textured fabrics.
party.female: Western Dress or Chic Separates. Focus: Modern silhouettes and trendy accessories.
